annotations.label: Inversive geometry

anchor.snippet: inversive geometry is the study of inversion

annotations.note: Mathlib defines sphere inversion in affine inner-product spaces, but not inversive geometry as a named geometry.

annotations.label: Inversion maps generalized circles

anchor.snippet: maps circles or lines to other circles or lines

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a sphere through the inversion center maps to a hyperplane, but not the full generalized-circle preservation theorem.

annotations.label: Inversion preserves crossing angles

anchor.snippet: preserves the angles between crossing curves

annotations.note: Mathlib gives the derivative of inversion as a scalar times a reflection, but no theorem states angle preservation for inverse curves.

annotations.label: Higher-dimensional inversion

anchor.snippet: The concept of inversion can be generalized to higher-dimensional spaces

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.inversion is defined for any affine space over a real inner-product vector space, covering higher dimensions.

annotations.label: Inverse point

anchor.snippet: the inverse of a point P with respect to a reference circle

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.inversion gives the inverse point by the standard center-radius distance formula.

annotations.label: Circle inversion

anchor.snippet: This is called circle inversion or plane inversion

annotations.note: The general sphere inversion specializes to plane circle inversion, although Mathlib has no separate circle-inversion alias.

annotations.label: Inversion is involutive

anchor.snippet: the result of applying the same inversion twice is the identity transformation

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.inversion_involutive proves that inversion with nonzero radius is involutive.

annotations.label: Inversion at infinity

anchor.snippet: it is necessary to introduce a point at infinity

annotations.note: Mathlib has a one-point compactification point at infinity, but EuclideanGeometry.inversion is not extended to swap the center with infinity.

annotations.label: Inside outside inversion

anchor.snippet: the inversion of any point inside the reference circle must lie outside it

annotations.note: The distance formula gives the reciprocal radial distance needed for inside-outside behavior, but the inequality statement is not named.

annotations.label: Circle points fixed by inversion

anchor.snippet: any point on the circle is unaffected

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.inversion_of_mem_sphere proves that every point on the reference sphere is fixed.

annotations.label: Distance monotonicity inside circle

anchor.snippet: the nearer the point to the center, the further away its transformation

annotations.note: The reciprocal distance formula is present, but monotonicity for points inside the circle is not packaged as a theorem.

annotations.label: Distance monotonicity near circle

anchor.snippet: the nearer the point to the circle, the closer its transformation

annotations.note: The reciprocal distance formula is present, but closeness-to-circle monotonicity is not packaged as a theorem.

annotations.label: Construct inverse outside circle

anchor.snippet: To construct the inverse P ' of a point P outside a circle Ø

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this compass-and-straightedge construction.

annotations.label: Construct inverse inside circle

anchor.snippet: To construct the inverse P of a point P ' inside a circle Ø

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this compass-and-straightedge construction.

annotations.label: Dutta inverse construction

anchor.snippet: There is a construction of the inverse point to A with respect to a circle Ø

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing Dutta's inverse-point construction.

annotations.label: Dutta construction correctness

anchor.snippet: A ' is the inverse point of A with respect to circle Ø

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing correctness of Dutta's construction.

annotations.label: Circle through center inverse

anchor.snippet: The inverse, with respect to the red circle, of a circle going through O

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that a sphere through the center maps to a hyperplane, with the center inserted because inversion fixes it.

annotations.label: Circle away from center inverse

anchor.snippet: The inverse, with respect to the red circle, of a circle not going through O

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the inverse image of a hyperplane is a sphere through the center, but not the circle-away-from-center image theorem.

annotations.label: Circle center not generally mapped to image center

anchor.snippet: does not map the center of the circle to the center of its image

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib stating the center behavior for the inverse image of a noncentral circle.

annotations.label: Inversion of set of points

anchor.snippet: The inversion of a set of points in the plane with respect to a circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has the point map used with ordinary Set.image, but no separate definition for inversion of a set of points.

annotations.label: Circle through center maps to line

anchor.snippet: A circle that passes through the center O of the reference circle inverts to a line

annotations.note: The theorem maps a sphere through the center to a hyperplane, which specializes in the plane to a circle mapping to a line.

annotations.label: Line through center fixed as set

anchor.snippet: a line passing through O is inverted into itself

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.image_inversion_affineSubspace_of_mem proves any affine subspace through the center is fixed as a set.

annotations.label: Circle away from center maps to circle

anchor.snippet: A circle not passing through O inverts to a circle not passing through O

annotations.note: Mathlib has hyperplane-to-sphere image results but no theorem for a circle not through the center mapping to another circle.

annotations.label: Reference intersection points invariant

anchor.snippet: these invariant points of intersection are also on the inverse circle

annotations.note: The fixed-point theorem covers points on the reference sphere, but the inverse-circle intersection statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Orthogonal generalized circles fixed by inversion

anchor.snippet: unchanged by inversion if and only if it is orthogonal to the reference circle

annotations.note: Sphere.orthRadius provides polar and tangent infrastructure related to orthogonality, but the iff invariant-generalized-circle theorem is absent.

annotations.label: Circle through inverse points orthogonal

anchor.snippet: If a circle q passes through two distinct points A and A'

annotations.note: The polar subspace infrastructure is present, but no theorem states that a circle through inverse points is orthogonal to the reference circle.

annotations.label: Orthogonal circle cuts inverse points on center line

anchor.snippet: If the circles k and q are orthogonal

annotations.note: The orthogonal-radius API is related, but the stated cutting property for orthogonal circles is not formalized.

annotations.label: Triangle inverse points relation

anchor.snippet: Given a triangle OAB in which O is the center of a circle k

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the distance relation between two inverse images, but not the full triangle formulation from the article.

annotations.label: Orthogonal circle intersections are inverse

anchor.snippet: The points of intersection of two circles p and q orthogonal to a circle k

annotations.note: Mathlib has polar and tangent infrastructure for spheres, but not this intersection-inverse theorem.

annotations.label: Tangents at inverse curve points

anchor.snippet: If M and M' are inverse points with respect to a circle k

annotations.note: The derivative of inversion is formalized, but no theorem relates tangent lines of inverse curves at inverse points.

annotations.label: Inversion preserves angle measure

anchor.snippet: Inversion leaves the measure of angles unaltered

annotations.note: The derivative formula plus conformal-angle infrastructure are present, but inversion angle preservation is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Inversion reverses oriented angle orientation

anchor.snippet: reverses the orientation of oriented angles

annotations.note: The derivative is a scalar times a reflection, but Mathlib has no theorem that inversion reverses oriented angles.

annotations.label: Line inversion

anchor.snippet: Inversion of a line is a circle containing the center of inversion

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that inversion maps a suitable hyperplane to a sphere through the center, specializing to line inversion in the plane.

annotations.label: Circle inversion

anchor.snippet: Inversion of a circle is another circle

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the circle-through-center case as a hyperplane image, but not the general circle-to-circle example.

annotations.label: Parabola inversion

anchor.snippet: Inversion of a parabola is a cardioid

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing inversion of a parabola to a cardioid.

annotations.label: Hyperbola inversion

anchor.snippet: Inversion of hyperbola is a lemniscate of Bernoulli

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing inversion of a hyperbola to a Bernoulli lemniscate.

annotations.label: Circle image center collinearity

anchor.snippet: the center of the circle being inverted and the center of its image under inversion are collinear

annotations.note: Mathlib proves inverse points lie on the radial line from the center, but no theorem gives the center of an inverted circle.

annotations.label: Intouch Euler line equals OI line

anchor.snippet: the Euler line of the intouch triangle of a triangle coincides with its OI line

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing the intouch triangle Euler-line statement.

annotations.label: Intouch triangle inversion proof

anchor.snippet: Invert with respect to the incircle of triangle ABC

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this intouch-triangle inversion proof.

annotations.label: Medial intouch triangle inversion

anchor.snippet: The medial triangle of the intouch triangle is inverted into triangle ABC

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing the medial-intouch inversion theorem.

annotations.label: Nine-point incenter circumcenter collinear

anchor.snippet: the nine-point center of the intouch triangle, the incenter and circumcenter of triangle ABC are collinear

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this intouch-triangle nine-point collinearity theorem.

annotations.label: Non-intersecting circles invert to concentric circles

anchor.snippet: Any two non-intersecting circles may be inverted into concentric circles

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing inversion of nonintersecting circles to concentric circles.

annotations.label: Inversive distance

anchor.snippet: the inversive distance (usually denoted δ) is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing inversive distance.

annotations.label: Non-intersecting circles invert to congruent circles

anchor.snippet: any two non-intersecting circles may be inverted into congruent circles

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing inversion of nonintersecting circles to congruent circles.

annotations.label: Peaucellier-Lipkin linkage

anchor.snippet: The Peaucellier–Lipkin linkage is a mechanical implementation of inversion in a circle

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing the Peaucellier-Lipkin linkage.

annotations.label: Peaucellier-Lipkin exact conversion

anchor.snippet: It provides an exact solution to the important problem of converting between linear and circular motion

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing the Peaucellier-Lipkin motion-conversion theorem.

annotations.label: Polar of inverse point

anchor.snippet: the lines perpendicular to the line PR through one of the points is the polar of the other point

annotations.note: Sphere.orthRadius is documented as the polar of the inversion of a point, but the article pole-polar definition is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Pole-polar incidence duality

anchor.snippet: If a point P lies on a line l

annotations.note: The orthRadius API gives a polar-like affine subspace, but the pole-polar incidence duality theorem is absent.

annotations.label: Moving point polar rotates about pole

anchor.snippet: If a point P moves along a line l

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this pole-polar motion statement.

annotations.label: Polar through tangent points

anchor.snippet: If two tangent lines can be drawn from a pole to the circle

annotations.note: Mathlib defines tangent subspaces from a point, but no theorem states that the polar passes through the tangent points.

annotations.label: Circle point polar is tangent

anchor.snippet: If a point lies on the circle, its polar is the tangent through this point

annotations.note: The theorem states that the orthRadius subspace is tangent exactly when the point lies on the sphere.

annotations.label: Point on own polar lies on circle

anchor.snippet: If a point P lies on its own polar line, then P is on the circle

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this pole-polar characterization.

annotations.label: Unique pole for each line

anchor.snippet: Each line has exactly one pole

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing uniqueness of poles for lines.

annotations.label: Sphere inversion

anchor.snippet: Circle inversion is generalizable to sphere inversion in three dimensions

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.inversion is defined for inversion in a sphere in arbitrary affine inner-product spaces.

annotations.label: Inverse point in 3D

anchor.snippet: The inversion of a point P in 3D with respect to a reference sphere

annotations.note: The same inversion definition specializes to three-dimensional Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Sphere maps to sphere

anchor.snippet: a sphere inverts to a sphere

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the sphere-through-center case mapping to a hyperplane, but not the general sphere-to-sphere image theorem.

annotations.label: Sphere through center maps to plane

anchor.snippet: if a sphere passes through the center O of the reference sphere

annotations.note: The theorem maps a sphere through the center to a hyperplane, specializing in 3D to a plane.

annotations.label: Plane through center maps to tangent sphere

anchor.snippet: Any plane passing through O , inverts to a sphere touching at O

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a hyperplane of the perpendicular-bisector form maps to a sphere through the center, but not this plane-through-center formulation.

annotations.label: Circle maps to circle in 3D inversion

anchor.snippet: A circle, that is, the intersection of a sphere with a secant plane, inverts into a circle

annotations.note: Mathlib has sphere-to-hyperplane image theorems, but not a theorem about intersections forming circles under 3D inversion.

annotations.label: Circle through center maps to line in 3D

anchor.snippet: if the circle passes through O it inverts into a line

annotations.note: The sphere-through-center image theorem is one component of the result, but Mathlib has no packaged 3D circle-to-line theorem.

annotations.label: 2D reduction for secant plane through center

anchor.snippet: This reduces to the 2D case when the secant plane passes through O

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this dimensional reduction statement.

annotations.label: Sphere inversion example

anchor.snippet: a non trivial inversion (the center of the sphere is not the center of inversion) of a sphere

annotations.note: The inversion map is formalized, but no specific nontrivial sphere-inversion example is present.

annotations.label: Cylinder cone torus invert to Dupin cyclide

anchor.snippet: The inversion of a cylinder, cone, or torus results in a Dupin cyclide

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing inversion of cylinders, cones, or tori to Dupin cyclides.

annotations.label: Spheroid

anchor.snippet: A spheroid is a surface of revolution

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing spheroids as surfaces of revolution.

annotations.label: Spheroid circle pencil maps to circle pencil

anchor.snippet: contains a pencil of circles which is mapped onto a pencil of circles

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this spheroid circle-pencil statement.

annotations.label: Spheroid inverse degree

anchor.snippet: The inverse image of a spheroid is a surface of degree 4

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing the degree of the inverse image of a spheroid.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid of one sheet

anchor.snippet: A hyperboloid of one sheet, which is a surface of revolution

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing a hyperboloid of one sheet as a surface of revolution.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid circle pencil maps to circle pencil

anchor.snippet: contains a pencil of circles which is mapped onto a pencil of circles

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this hyperboloid circle-pencil statement.

annotations.label: Hyperboloid line pencils map to circle pencils

anchor.snippet: contains additional two pencils of lines, which are mapped onto pencils of circles

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this hyperboloid line-pencil statement.

annotations.label: Stereographic projection

anchor.section: Stereographic projection as the inversion of a sphere
anchor.snippet: A stereographic projection usually projects a sphere

annotations.note: The stereographic open partial homeomorphism from the unit sphere to an orthogonal hyperplane is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stereographic projection by inversion

anchor.section: Stereographic projection as the inversion of a sphere
anchor.snippet: This mapping can be performed by an inversion of the sphere onto its tangent plane

annotations.note: Mathlib defines stereographic projection, but the file explicitly leaves relating it to inversion as a TODO.

annotations.label: Sphere maps to tangent plane under unit inversion

anchor.section: Stereographic projection as the inversion of a sphere
anchor.snippet: then it will be mapped by the inversion at the unit sphere (red) onto the tangent plane

annotations.note: Mathlib has a sphere-through-center to hyperplane theorem, but not this unit-inversion tangent-plane formulation.

annotations.label: Projection lines fixed under inversion

anchor.section: Stereographic projection as the inversion of a sphere
anchor.snippet: The lines through the center of inversion

annotations.note: Any affine subspace through the center, including a projection line, is fixed as a set under inversion.

annotations.label: 6-sphere coordinates

anchor.snippet: The 6-sphere coordinates are a coordinate system for three-dimensional space

annotations.label: Generalized circles as blocks

anchor.snippet: the generalized circles are called "blocks"

anchor.snippet: exist in both finite and infinite versions

annotations.label: Riemann sphere model

anchor.snippet: A model for the Möbius plane that comes from the Euclidean plane is the Riemann sphere

annotations.label: Cross-ratio invariant under inversion

anchor.snippet: The cross-ratio between 4 points

anchor.snippet: length of the line

annotations.label: Two concentric inversions give dilation

anchor.snippet: The combination of two inversions in concentric circles results in a similarity

annotations.note: Mathlib relates inversions with concentric radii by a homothety, but does not state the two-inversion composition theorem directly.

annotations.label: Möbius group elements conformal

anchor.snippet: Möbius group elements are analytic functions of the whole plane and so are necessarily conformal

annotations.note: Mathlib proves holomorphic maps with nonzero derivative are conformal and defines fractional linear actions, but not this global Möbius-group statement.

anchor.snippet: showing that the

annotations.label: Point at infinity for circle inversion

annotations.label: Complex projective line as Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: the complex projective line , often called the Riemann sphere

annotations.note: Mathlib identifies OnePoint K with the projectivization of K^2, but does not name the complex case as the Riemann sphere.

annotations.label: Inversive geometry includes Lobachevsky-Bolyai ideas

anchor.snippet: inversive geometry includes the ideas originated by Lobachevsky and Bolyai

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this historical inclusion claim.

annotations.label: Geometry as space with transformation group

anchor.snippet: the term geometry for a space together with a group of mappings of that space

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing geometry as a space together with a transformation group.

annotations.label: Geometric properties are group invariants

anchor.snippet: The significant properties of figures in the geometry are those that are invariant under this group

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing this Erlangen-style invariance statement for inversive geometry.

annotations.label: Higher-dimensional sphere inversion

anchor.snippet: an inversion in the sphere of radius r centered at the point

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.inversion is the higher-dimensional sphere inversion map in affine inner-product spaces.

annotations.label: Inversions generate Euclidean similarities

anchor.snippet: The transformation by inversion in hyperplanes or hyperspheres in E n can be used to generate dilations, translations, or rotations

annotations.note: Mathlib has inversion-homothety infrastructure, but not a generation theorem for Euclidean similarities.

annotations.label: Concentric hypersphere inversions give dilation

anchor.snippet: two concentric hyperspheres, used to produce successive inversions, result in a dilation

annotations.note: The inversion_mul theorem is the relevant concentric-radius homothety relation, but the composition result is not named.

annotations.label: Parallel hyperplane reflections give translation

anchor.snippet: When two parallel hyperplanes are used to produce successive reflections, the result is a translation

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Euclidean reflection in affine subspaces, but no theorem states that two parallel hyperplane reflections compose to a translation.

annotations.label: Intersecting hyperplane reflections give rotation

anchor.snippet: When two hyperplanes intersect in an ( n −2)- flat

annotations.note: Mathlib has algebraic formulas for products of reflections, but not the Euclidean intersecting-hyperplane rotation theorem.

annotations.label: Isometry

anchor.snippet: Any combination of reflections, translations, and rotations is called an isometry

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Isometry as a distance-preserving map between metric spaces.

annotations.label: Similarity

anchor.snippet: Any combination of reflections, dilations, translations, and rotations is a similarity

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes metric similarities as Dilation maps that scale distances by a nonzero ratio.

annotations.label: Similarities are conformal maps

anchor.snippet: All of these are conformal maps

annotations.note: Mathlib defines conformal maps and metric dilations, but no theorem states that similarities are conformal.

annotations.label: Liouville conformal maps theorem

anchor.snippet: where the space has three or more dimensions, the mappings generated by inversion are the only conformal mappings

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing Liouville theorem for higher-dimensional conformal maps.

annotations.label: Point at infinity identifies hyperplanes and hyperspheres

anchor.snippet: The addition of a point at infinity to the space obviates the distinction between hyperplane and hypersphere

annotations.note: Mathlib has one-point compactification and inversion image results, but no theorem identifying hyperplanes with hyperspheres via infinity.

annotations.label: Möbius transformations

anchor.snippet: The transformations of inversive geometry are often referred to as Möbius transformations

annotations.note: Mathlib defines fractional linear transformations on the upper half-plane, but not general inversive-geometry Möbius transformations.

annotations.label: Anticonformal map

anchor.snippet: The circle inversion map is anticonformal

annotations.note: No declaration named or defining anticonformal maps was found under Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conformal map

anchor.snippet: a map is called conformal if it preserves oriented angles

annotations.label: Algebraic anticonformal condition

anchor.snippet: Algebraically, a map is anticonformal if at every point the Jacobian is a scalar times an orthogonal matrix with negative determinant

annotations.note: No declaration was found under Mathlib formalizing the negative-determinant Jacobian condition for anticonformality.

annotations.label: Inversive map is anticonformal by Jacobian

annotations.label: Circle inversion as conjugate inverse

annotations.label: Complex inverse conformal

anchor.snippet: The complex analytic inverse map is conformal

annotations.label: Circle inversion anticonformal in complex plane

anchor.snippet: its conjugate, circle inversion, is anticonformal

annotations.note: The complex reciprocal and conjugation formulas are present, but no anticonformality theorem for circle inversion is formalized.

annotations.label: Homographies and anti-homographies

anchor.snippet: a homography is conformal while an anti-homography is anticonformal

annotations.note: Mathlib defines homography-like fractional linear transformations on the upper half-plane, but not the homography versus anti-homography conformality statement.

annotations.label: Poincare disk hypersurfaces

anchor.snippet: the hypersurfaces of the Poincaré disk model of hyperbolic geometry

annotations.label: Unit sphere inversion swaps inside and outside
